items.text: Establish last known well or symptom onset, progression, baseline cognition, baseline neurologic function, and functional status.
Ask about weakness, numbness, facial asymmetry, speech or vision change, imbalance, dysphagia, and severe headache.
Ask about loss of consciousness, seizure-like activity, prodrome, post-event confusion, incontinence, tongue injury, and witness observations.
Ask about falls, head or neck injury, anticoagulant or antiplatelet use, and bleeding symptoms.
Ask about recent medication changes, sedatives, alcohol, recreational substances, withdrawal risk, and toxic exposures.
Ask about fever, poor intake, glucose symptoms, renal or liver disease, and other systemic symptoms that may affect cognition.
Ask witnesses about onset, focal features, duration, eye deviation, movements, responsiveness, injuries, recovery, and post-event confusion.
Ask about aura, tongue injury, incontinence, automatisms, prior episodes, known epilepsy, and medication adherence.
Ask about fever, head trauma, infection symptoms, sleep deprivation, alcohol or benzodiazepine withdrawal, substances, and glucose symptoms.
Ask about headache, focal deficits, cancer, stroke, pregnancy when relevant, family history, and prior central nervous system disease.
Assess vital signs, general appearance, glucose status if available at bedside, and signs of cardiorespiratory instability.
Assess level of consciousness, attention, orientation, language, memory, behavior, and fluctuation from baseline.
Assess pupils, visual fields when feasible, ocular movements, facial symmetry, speech, palate, and swallowing safety as indicated.
Assess pronator drift, limb strength, tone, sensation, reflex asymmetry, and neglect when appropriate.
Assess coordination, truncal stability, gait, and Romberg only when it is safe to do so.
Inspect for head or neck injury and assess neck stiffness or rash when the presentation makes this relevant.
Assess arousal, attention, orientation, language, behavior, memory, and return toward baseline.
Assess pupils, gaze, facial symmetry, strength, sensation, reflexes, coordination, and gait only when safe.
Inspect scalp, face, tongue, shoulders, skin, and extremities for injury or tongue bite.
Assess temperature, neck stiffness, rash, tremor, diaphoresis, pupils, and signs of intoxication or withdrawal.
